content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 26, 1993

James C. Johnson
2435 Calle Almonte
Santa Barbara, CA 93109

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-070

Dear Mr. Johnson:

This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

This advice is limited to the provisions of the Act. Other
legal provisions, such as Government Code Section 1090 or the
general doctrine of incompatible offices, may have some bearing on
this situation. It is suggested that you contact the city
attorney and county counsel for advice as to these matters.

QUESTIONS

1. Are you permitted to participate in decisions on a
bikeway and street widening project located 800 feet from your
rental home?

2. Are you permitted to participate in decisions for a 45-
unit residential subdivision located 1,200 feet from your
residence?

3. Are you permitted to participate in decisions to widen a
portion of U.S. Highway 101 located about two miles from your
interests in real property?

_______________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Two


CONCLUSIONS

1. You are not permitted to participate in the bikeway and
street widening project if it will increase or decrease the fair
market value of your interests in real property by $10,000, or its
rental value by $1,000 or more per 12-month period.

2. You are not permitted to participate in the 45-unit
residential subdivision project if the decision will increase or
decrease the fair market value of your residence by $10,000 or
more.

3. You are permitted to participate in the highway widening
project unless the decision would be unusual enough to increase
the value of the property by $10,000.

FACTS

You are a member of the City of Santa Barbara Planning
Commission. In addition, you are a manager/supervisor with the
California Coastal Commission. In your capacity with the Coastal
Commission, you work on planning and regulatory matters in the
South Coast Coast office for all jurisdictions, except the City
of Santa Barbara.

You own two homes in the City of Santa Barbara. One is used
as your principal place of residence, the other is a rental home.
There are three projects on future agendas for the planning
commission regarding development of areas located near your home
or near your rental.

The first project is a request for a coastal development
permit for the addition of bikeways and the widening of the 1,100
to 1,400 blocks of Shoreline Drive in the City of Santa Barbara.
This project is located within 800 feet of your rental home.

The second project is a request for a coastal permit for a
45-unit residential subdivision on vacant ocean blufftop and
hillside parcels known as the Wilcox property near the
intersection of Cliff Drive and Las Positas Road. The northeast
corner of the property is located within approximately 1,200 feet
of the parcel your residence is located on.

The third project is a planning/policy amendment and coastal
permit for a Caltrans project to widen a portion of U.S. Highway
101 through the eastern portion of the city, about two miles from
your nearest home. This project also crosses the city boundary
into the County of Santa Barbara east along the roadway route to
the City of Carpinteria. You have worked on a planning matter for
the portion of this project located in the County of Santa Barbara
in your Coastal Commission capacity. This work for the state does
not include the portion of the project in the City of Santa
Barbara.

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Three


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

Under Section 87103, an official has a financial interest in
a governmental decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Accordingly, you may not make or participate in the making,
or use your official position to influence any decision which
would have a reasonably foreseeable2/ material financial effect on
your real property interests.3/

The Commission has developed regulations establishing
standards to determine when a decision would have a material
financial effect on an official's financial interests. If your
property was directly affected by the decision, Regulation 18702.1
would be the appropriate standard to determine materiality. This
is not the case in your situation.

Your real property interests will be indirectly affected by
the decisions of the planning commission concerning each of the
projects. Regulation 18702.3 provides standards for real property
interests which are indirectly affected by an official's decision.
That regulation provides that a decision on an official's real
property interest is material if:

(1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the

2/ Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

3/ Salary from a governmental source is exempt from the
definition of "income." Thus, the State of California is not a
disqualifying source of income to you. (Section 82030.)

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Three


boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

     (2)  The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

     (3)  The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

          (A)  Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or

          (B)  Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.

               (Regulation 18702.3(a).)

1.  Coastal Development Permit for Bikeways and Street Widening

     This project is located within 800 feet of your rental home.
You are not permitted to participate in decisions concerning this
project if it will foreseeably increase or decrease the fair
market value of that home by $10,000, or affect the rental value
by $1,000 or more per 12-month period. (Regulation
18702.3(a)(3).)

     You indicated that you were not aware of how the approval or
denial of this project will affect the value of the home.  The
Commission is not the finder of fact in rendering advice.  Public
officials must make a good faith effort to determine the effect of
a particular decision. 4/




4/  Please note, later in this letter we will discuss facts which
you should take into consideration to determine material financial
effect.

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Five


2.  Coastal Permit for 45-Unit Residential Subdivision

     This project will add approximately 45 residential units on
vacant ocean blufftop and hillside parcels. It is located within
1,200 feet of your residence. Regulation 18702.3(a)(3)(A) would
apply to this decision as well. If the decision will foreseeably
increase or decrease the fair market value of your home by
$10,000, you are not permitted to participate in the decision.

3.  Coastal Permit to Widen a Portion of U.S. Highway 101

     This project would widen a portion of U.S. Highway 101
through the eastern portion of the city and is located about two
miles from your interests in real property. As a member of the
planning commission you will soon be preparing a coastal plan
amendment working with the other members of the planning
commission to address the highway widening project.

     For real property interests beyond 2,500 feet, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that a decision could affect the real
property interests unless:

          (1)  There are specific circumstances regarding the
decision, its effect, and the nature of the real
property which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
fair market value or the rental value of the real
property will be affected by the amounts set forth above
in Regulation 18702.3(a)(3); and

               (2)  Either of the following apply:

                    (A)  The effect will not be substantially
the same as the effect upon at least 25
percent of all the properties which are
within a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries
of the real property in which the official has
an interest; or

                    (B)  There are not at least 10 properties
under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot
radius of the property in which the official
has an interest.

                              (Regulation 18702.3(b).)

     Essentially, this means that if you have determined that the
decision has such a significant impact that your real property
could increase or decrease by $10,000 in value, you still may
participate in the decision so long as 25 percent of the
properties within 2,500 of your real property were affected
similarly and the real property within 2,500 feet is owned by at
least 10 property owners.

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Six


You have not provided facts to suggest that the decisions
implementing the widening of U.S. 101 would be unusual enough to
increase the value of your property by $10,000.

Factors to be Considered in Determining Material Financial Effect

You indicated that you were unsure of the effect these
decisions would have on your real property interests. The
Commission cannot determine whether there will be a material
financial effect on your property. You must make a reasonable
good faith effort to determine the financial effects of a decision
on your interests in real property. (McLaughlin Advice Letter,
No. I-91-141, Wallace Advice Letter, A-93-071.)

Factors which must be considered in determining whether a
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on
your real property include, but are not limited to:

     (1)  The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship to the
property in which the official has an interest;

     (2)  Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will affect the development potential or income
producing potential of the property;

     (3)  In addition to the foregoing, in the case of
residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to
the character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity
of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood.

(Regulation 18702.3(d).)

If you have made a good faith effort, utilizing the factors
in Regulation 18702.3, and determined that your real property
interests will not be materially affected by your decisions, you
would not be prohibited from participating in the decisions
discussed above.

Using Your Official Position to Influence

In our telephone conversation, you indicated that you were
concerned about your position with the California Coastal
Commission in relation to these projects. You indicated that your
planning and supervisory activities with the Coastal Commission
within Santa Barbara County, exclude any projects located within
the City of Santa Barbara. Another Coastal Commission employee,
who is not under your supervision or control, is responsible for
planning functions within the City of Santa Barbara.

Advice File No. A-93-070
Page Seven


Section 87103 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

Thus, you could not use your Coastal Commission position to
influence the City of Santa Barbara with respect to decisions that
have a material financial effect on your property by purporting to
act on behalf of, or as the representative of, your agency to any
member, officer, employee or consultant an agency. (Regulation
18702.1(c).) In other words, you could not make a presentation
before the planning commission purporting to be a representative
of the Coastal Commission to influence a decision that will have a
material financial effect on your property.

I hope this answers your questions sufficiently. If you need
further assistance, please feel free to contact the Commission's
Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

JM/JET/jt
